perturbation.original_text: Unless a particular portion of Proprietary Information becomes nonproprietary as provided in Section 4(a) hereof, or unless otherwise authorized in a written agreement signed by an authorized officer of STI, Pinetree has a duty to protect each particular portion of Proprietary Information in for a period of five (5) years from the date of this Agreement, provided that Proprietary Information which constitutes a trade secret under applicable law shall be protected as long as the Proprietary Information qualifies as a trade secret under Maryland law.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless a particular portion of Proprietary Information becomes nonproprietary as provided in Section 4(a) hereof, or unless otherwise authorized in a written agreement signed by an authorized officer of STI, Pinetree has a duty to protect each particular portion of Proprietary Information for a period of one (1) year from the date of this Agreement, provided that Proprietary Information which constitutes a trade secret under applicable law shall be protected as long as the Proprietary Information qualifies as a trade secret under Maryland law.
perturbation.explanation: The change reduces the protection period for proprietary information from five years to one year, which may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under Maryland law, creating a legal contradiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: Reducing the protection period to one year may not provide sufficient time to prevent misappropriation and harm from the use of trade secrets, conflicting with the reasonable measures required to maintain secrecy under Maryland law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Maryland Commercial Law Code Section 11-1205 (2022) - Preservation of Trade Secret During Action :: 2022 Maryland Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Maryland Code 2023 Maryland Code 2022 Maryland Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Maryland Statutes Commercial Law Title 11 - Trade Regulation Subtitle 12 - Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act Section 11-1205 - Preservation of Trade Secret During Action Universal Citation: MD Commercial Law Code § 11-1205 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next In an action under this subtitle, a court shall preserve the secrecy of an alleged trade secret by reasonable means, which may include granting protective orders in connection with discovery proceedings, holding in-camera hearings, sealing the records of the action, and ordering any person involved in the litigation not to disclose an alleged trade secret without prior court approval. perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the protection period for proprietary information from five years to one year. The Perturbation Explanation states this may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under Maryland law. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that reducing the protection period may not provide sufficient time to prevent misappropriation and harm from the use of trade secrets, potentially conflicting with reasonable measures required to maintain secrecy under Maryland law. Scraped Law Snippet 2, Maryland Commercial Law Code Section 11-1205, discusses the preservation of trade secrets during action, implying an expectation of ongoing protection, which could be undermined by the reduced one-year period, particularly if the trade secret's value extends beyond that timeframe.

perturbation.original_text: Pinetree must protect the Proprietary Information from unauthorized use or disclosure by exercising the same degree of care that Pinetree uses with respect to information of its own of a similar, highly valuable nature, and Pinetree must, at minimum, use at least reasonable care.
perturbation.changed_text: Pinetree must protect the Proprietary Information from unauthorized use or disclosure by exercising minimal care.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the required degree of care to 'minimal care' for Proprietary Information contradicts the standard of reasonable care required to protect trade secrets under Maryland law. This creates a legal contradiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: Defining 'reasonable care' in the context of trade secrets misappropriation requires more than minimal care. Reducing the standard to 'minimal care' fails to meet the requirements for maintaining trade secret protection, thus contradicting Maryland law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Maryland Commercial Law Code Section 11-1201 (2022) - Definitions :: 2022 Maryland Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Maryland Code 2023 Maryland Code 2022 Maryland Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Maryland Statutes Commercial Law Title 11 - Trade Regulation Subtitle 12 - Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act Section 11-1201 - Definitions Universal Citation: MD Commercial Law Code § 11-1201 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Next (a) In this subtitle the following words have the meanings indicated. (b) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (c) “Misappropriation” means the: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the person’s knowledge of the trade secret was: 1. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; 2. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Before a material change of the person’s position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (d) “Person” means an individual, corporation, business trust, statutory trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or a
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates a degree of care for Proprietary Information that is 'the same degree of care that Pinetree uses with respect to information of its own of a similar, highly valuable nature, and Pinetree must, at minimum, use at least reasonable care.' The changed text reduces this to 'minimal care.' The Law Explanation states that 'reasonable care' requires more than minimal care to maintain trade secret protection, thus failing to meet the requirements and contradicting Maryland law. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines 'misappropriation' which involves the 'acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means' or 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent'. Reducing the standard of care to 'minimal' increases the risk of misappropriation. Therefore, the modified text contradicts the reasonable care standard required to protect trade secrets, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: At any time upon STI's request, Pinetree shall promptly redeliver to STI, or destroy at STI's direction, all written material in any media or format whatsoever containing, reflecting or in any way derived from the Proprietary Information (including notes, summaries, copies, extracts or other reproductions, in whole or in part), regardless of whether the material was prepared by STI or on STI's behalf.
perturbation.changed_text: At any time upon STI's request with 90 days of advanced notice, Pinetree shall redeliver to STI, or destroy at STI's direction, all written material in any media or format whatsoever containing, reflecting or in any way derived from the Proprietary Information (including notes, summaries, copies, extracts or other reproductions, in whole or in part), regardless of whether the material was prepared by STI or on STI's behalf.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring STI to provide 90 days' notice before requesting the return or destruction of proprietary information is an unreasonable delay. Such notice is a de facto appropriation of a trade secret, particularly when the nature of the information and potential harm from disclosure can be time-sensitive. It goes against the duty to protect trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: By requiring a 90-day advance notice for the return/destruction of Proprietary Information, the modified contract term enables potential misappropriation during the notification period. This contradicts the Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act, which seeks to prevent the unauthorized use or disclosure of trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Maryland Commercial Law Code Section 11-1202 (2022) - Misappropriation of Trade Secrets May Be Enjoined :: 2022 Maryland Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Maryland Code 2023 Maryland Code 2022 Maryland Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Maryland Statutes Commercial Law Title 11 - Trade Regulation Subtitle 12 - Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act Section 11-1202 - Misappropriation of Trade Secrets May Be Enjoined Universal Citation: MD Commercial Law Code § 11-1202 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. (b) Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (c) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. (d) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. (e) In this section, “exceptional circumstances” includes a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. Previous Next Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. Maryland may have more current or accurate information. Please check official sources. This site is protected by reCAPTCHA and the Google Privacy Policy and Terms of Service a
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces a 90-day notice period before Pinetree is required to return or destroy proprietary information, potentially enabling misappropriation during that period. This contradicts the Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act. Scraped Law Snippet 2, MD Commercial Law Code § 11-1202 (a), states that "actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined." The 90-day notice provides an opportunity for threatened misappropriation, undermining the protection the law aims to provide.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Maryland, without regarding to its choice of law provisions. The parties further agree and consent to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of Maryland; thus, in the event that either party initiates an action pursuant to or otherwise governed by this Agreement, the exclusive jurisdiction of such action shall be in Maryland.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California, without regarding to its choice of law provisions. The parties further agree and consent to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of California; thus, in the event that either party initiates an action pursuant to or otherwise governed by this Agreement, the exclusive jurisdiction of such action shall be in California.
perturbation.explanation: The original agreement specifies Maryland law and jurisdiction. This modification changes the governing law and jurisdiction to California. Since the contract involves a Maryland company (STI) and concerns the protection of trade secrets within Maryland, the change could improperly subject the interpretation and enforcement of trade secret rights to California law, which might offer different protections or remedies than Maryland law. This is especially problematic if the trade secrets are used or misappropriated in Maryland.
perturbation.law_explanation: Changing the governing law from Maryland to California, particularly in a contract concerning trade secrets, potentially undermines the protections afforded by the Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act. By subjecting disputes to California law, the contract could circumvent the specific provisions and remedies available under Maryland law for trade secret misappropriation occurring within Maryland.

perturbation.justification: The changed text substitutes Maryland law and jurisdiction with California law and jurisdiction. Given that the contract involves a Maryland company and concerns trade secrets, this shift could undermine the protections afforded by Maryland's Uniform Trade Secrets Act, as indicated in the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines 'Misappropriation' under Maryland Commercial Law Code, highlighting Maryland's specific legal framework for trade secret protection. By changing the governing law to California, the contract potentially circumvents the specific provisions and remedies available under Maryland law for trade secret misappropriation occurring within Maryland, thus contradicting the intended protection under Maryland law.
